summary: As display technology advances from HD to 4K to 8K, bandwidth requirements are growing exponentially—8K streaming requires 100mbps while average US speeds barely reach 96mbps. The 'last mile' problem makes fiber deployment prohibitively expensive, as Google Fiber discovered. Satellite internet from companies like Starlink and Astranis may finally solve the connectivity gap, delivering gigabit speeds without the infrastructure challenges of laying physical cable to every home.
key_points: 8K streaming requires 100mbps, but average US download speed is only 96.25mbps
The 'last mile' problem makes fiber deployment extremely expensive
Google Fiber failed because laying new fiber lines to homes was too costly
Satellite internet (Starlink, Astranis) may bypass the last mile problem entirely
Multiple devices sharing bandwidth means households actually need multiples of recommended speeds
money_quotes: If we know that the pixel density of televisions is only going to increase over time, causing streaming services to need more bandwidth, why are we not seeing a bunch of fiber being laid down or other innovations to get faster internet to the mass market?
The 'last mile' is the last bit of cable to get the connection to your home or business, these are known as drop cables.

Fiber is similar to public infrastructure like a freeway, you need to put in the investment upfront but it will pay off dividends over time. Most companies do not get that and want an economic return upfront.
The solution to the 'last mile' problem might be wireless, which leads us to the current innovations with satellites.
As the world continues to move from physical to digital at a rapid pace, we should see high bandwidth internet take a front and center role.
key_insight: Display technology is advancing faster than internet infrastructure can support it, and satellite internet may be the only economically viable path to universal high-bandwidth access.
